title: Unexplained Intraoperative Hypoxemia
summary: A 54-year-old woman under general anesthesia for a laparoscopic hysterectomy desaturates after the abdomen is insufflated and she is placed in steep Trendelenburg. Learners must run the systematic hypoxemia drill — 100% oxygen and hand ventilation, exclude the machine and circuit, work through the tube and lung differential — and discover that pneumoperitoneum and head-down positioning have migrated the endotracheal tube into the right mainstem bronchus, then correct it and re-expand the collapsed lung.
learningObjectives: Run a systematic response to intraoperative hypoxemia: 100% oxygen at high flow, hand-ventilate to feel compliance, and confirm oxygenation is real before chasing causes.

Exclude the machine and delivery system early — gas analyzer FiO2, a disconnected or misprogrammed circuit, and the SpO2 probe/waveform — and be ready to ventilate from a self-inflating bag on a separate oxygen source.
Work the tube-and-lung differential in order: mainstem intubation, mucus plug or kink, bronchospasm, aspiration, pneumothorax, pulmonary edema, and embolism.

Recognize that pneumoperitoneum plus steep Trendelenburg displaces the carina cephalad and migrates the endotracheal tube toward the right mainstem, and confirm it by unequal breath sounds and tube depth.
Correct a mainstem intubation by auscultating, withdrawing the tube to restore bilateral breath sounds, and re-securing it, then recruit the atelectatic lung.

Escalate appropriately: call for help, ask the surgeon to desufflate and level the table if hypoxemia persists, and know when a differential item (pneumothorax, embolism) demands a different intervention.
setup: OR mid-case for a laparoscopic hysterectomy: general endotracheal anesthesia, mechanical ventilation, abdomen about to be insufflated and the table about to go steep head-down.
The ETT was taped at 21 cm at the lip after an uncomplicated intubation with initially equal breath sounds — set this up so the depth is 'correct' before positioning.
Labeled props: a suction catheter for the ETT, a stethoscope, a self-inflating (Ambu) bag with a separate E-cylinder, and an oxygen analyzer readout the faculty can quote.

The simulated monitor does not display airway pressure — faculty should report peak inspiratory pressure ('rising to 34 cmH2O') and breath sounds ('absent on the left, present on the right') when learners auscultate.
Confederate surgeon who can confirm the insufflation pressure and Trendelenburg angle and can desufflate or level the table when asked.
Preop note: BMI 34, otherwise healthy; no cardiopulmonary disease — the point is that the cause is mechanical, not the patient.

patient.name: Renata Alvarez
pmh: Menorrhagia from fibroids, for laparoscopic hysterectomy

airway.notes: 7.0 ETT taped at 21 cm at the lip; grade 2 view; equal bilateral breath sounds documented after intubation.

patient.plan: Laparoscopic total hysterectomy under general endotracheal anesthesia; steep Trendelenburg with pneumoperitoneum for pelvic exposure.

phases.label: Stable Maintenance
phases.description: Uncomplicated laparoscopic case before positioning; equal breath sounds, SpO2 99%.

phases.label: Insufflation and Positioning
phases.description: Pneumoperitoneum and steep Trendelenburg shift the carina cephalad.

phases.label: Desaturation
phases.description: SpO2 drifts down with rising peak pressure — unexplained hypoxemia.

phases.label: Systematic Diagnosis
phases.description: Oxygen, hand ventilation, machine/circuit exclusion, and the tube-and-lung differential.

phases.label: Treatment
phases.description: Correct the mainstem intubation and re-expand the collapsed lung.

phases.label: Resolution
phases.description: Bilateral breath sounds restored; oxygenation recovers; secure and document.

events.label: Insufflation and steep Trendelenburg
events.description: The abdomen is insufflated to 15 mmHg and the table goes steep head-down. The diaphragm and carina move cephalad toward the fixed tube tip; peak pressures rise a little and compliance falls.

events.label: Gradual unexplained desaturation
events.description: SpO2 drifts from 99% into the low 90s over a couple of minutes with the ETT now in the right mainstem — the left lung is no longer ventilated. Faculty: 'peak pressure up to 34 cmH2O.' EtCO2 rises as dead space and shunt grow.

events.label: Hypoxemia worsens (unrecognized)
events.description: Left untreated the shunt deepens: SpO2 falls into the low 80s, the patient becomes tachycardic, and there is now real urgency. Fire if the mainstem is not found and corrected.

events.label: 100% oxygen and hand ventilation
events.description: FiO2 to 1.0 at high flow and switching to hand ventilation buys time and lets the team feel the poor, one-lung compliance directly. SpO2 improves modestly but the underlying problem remains.

events.label: Machine, circuit, and probe excluded
events.description: Gas analyzer confirms delivered FiO2, the circuit and capnograph show no disconnection, and the SpO2 waveform and probe are good. A marker step — it changes no vitals but rules out the delivery system and the reading.

events.label: Auscultate and suction the tube
events.description: Passing a suction catheter finds a patent tube with no plug; auscultation reveals the finding — 'breath sounds absent on the left, present on the right.' A marker step that localizes the problem to the tube position.

events.label: Endotracheal tube withdrawn to mid-trachea
events.description: Recognizing the right mainstem intubation, the tube is withdrawn a few centimeters until breath sounds return bilaterally and re-secured. The left lung is ventilated again and oxygenation begins to recover.

events.label: Recruitment and desufflation
events.description: A recruitment maneuver re-expands the atelectatic left lung, and asking the surgeon to briefly desufflate and lessen the head-down tilt improves ventilation further. SpO2 climbs toward normal.

events.label: Oxygenation restored
events.description: With the tube repositioned, the lung recruited, and PEEP optimized, SpO2 returns to 99% on a reduced FiO2 and peak pressures normalize. The team re-checks tube depth after any further position change and documents the event.

expectedActions.label: Increase FiO2 to 100% at high flow immediately
Switch to hand ventilation to assess compliance and confirm chest movement
Exclude the machine and circuit: gas-analyzer FiO2, disconnection, capnograph, SpO2 probe/waveform
expectedActions.description: Be ready to ventilate from a self-inflating bag on a separate oxygen source if the machine is in doubt.

expectedActions.label: Auscultate both lungs and check ETT depth; pass a suction catheter for patency
State a hypoxemia differential: mainstem intubation, plug/kink, bronchospasm, aspiration, pneumothorax, edema, embolism
Identify the right mainstem intubation from unequal breath sounds after Trendelenburg/insufflation
Withdraw the ETT until breath sounds are bilateral and re-secure it
Recruit the atelectatic lung and optimize PEEP; ask the surgeon to desufflate/level the table if needed
Call for help and communicate with the surgical team as hypoxemia persists
Re-check tube depth after position changes and document the event and plan

expectedProgression: The case is stable until the abdomen is insufflated and the table goes steep Trendelenburg, shifting the carina cephalad toward a tube that was taped at a correct depth.
Over the next couple of minutes SpO2 drifts into the low 90s with rising peak pressure — unexplained hypoxemia, not a patient with lung disease.
Learners should immediately go to 100% oxygen and hand-ventilate, then exclude the machine, circuit, and probe before touching the patient's physiology.
Working the differential, auscultation reveals absent left-sided breath sounds and the tube depth confirms a right mainstem intubation from the positional migration.
If the mainstem is not found, the shunt deepens and SpO2 falls into the low 80s with tachycardia — the prompt to escalate and call for help.
Withdrawing the tube until breath sounds are bilateral, recruiting the collapsed lung, and easing the insufflation/tilt restores oxygenation.
The team optimizes PEEP, re-checks tube depth after any further positioning, and documents the event.
correctManagement: Increase FiO2 to 1.0 at high fresh-gas flow the moment SpO2 falls, and switch to hand ventilation to feel compliance and confirm both sides move.

Exclude the delivery system first: confirm delivered FiO2 on the gas analyzer, rule out circuit disconnection and misprogramming on the capnograph, and check the SpO2 probe and waveform; be ready to ventilate from a self-inflating bag on a separate oxygen source.
Work the tube-and-lung differential systematically: mainstem intubation, mucus plug or kink, bronchospasm, aspiration, pneumothorax, pulmonary edema, and embolism.
Auscultate both lung fields and check the tube depth; passing a suction catheter confirms patency and excludes a plug.
Recognize that pneumoperitoneum and steep Trendelenburg migrate the tube toward the right mainstem — unequal breath sounds with a deep tube confirm it.
Withdraw the tube a few centimeters until breath sounds are bilateral and re-secure it at the new depth.
Recruit the atelectatic lung, optimize PEEP, and ask the surgeon to briefly desufflate and reduce the head-down tilt if oxygenation is slow to recover.
Call for help early if hypoxemia is severe or persistent, and re-check tube depth after any further position change; document the event and the plan.
commonErrors: Turning up FiO2 and waiting, without hand-ventilating or systematically searching for the cause.
Skipping the machine/circuit/probe exclusion and treating a delivery-system or artifact problem as patient hypoxemia (or vice versa).
Never auscultating — the single maneuver that localizes a mainstem intubation — or not re-checking tube depth after steep positioning and insufflation.
Anchoring on bronchospasm or 'the patient's obesity' and missing the mechanical, positional cause.
Advancing rather than withdrawing the tube, or failing to re-secure it at the corrected depth.
Forgetting to recruit the collapsed lung and optimize PEEP after correcting the tube, so oxygenation lags.
Not communicating with the surgeon about desufflation and table position while the patient is hypoxemic.

points: Intraoperative hypoxemia needs a systematic drill, not a reflex: 100% oxygen and hand ventilation first, then exclude the machine, circuit, and probe, then work the tube-and-lung differential in order.
The capnograph and airway pressure are part of the hypoxemia work-up: a disconnection shows on the CO2 trace, and a rising peak pressure with unilateral breath sounds points at the tube.

Pneumoperitoneum and steep Trendelenburg move the carina cephalad by roughly 1-2 cm, so a tube taped at a correct depth can migrate into the right mainstem after positioning — re-checking depth after position changes is prevention.
Auscultation is the decisive, immediately available test for mainstem intubation: absent breath sounds on one side with a deep tube. The fix is to withdraw, not advance.
A collapsed lung does not re-expand instantly: after repositioning the tube, a recruitment maneuver and optimized PEEP restore oxygenation, and easing insufflation and tilt helps.
The differential still matters — pneumothorax, aspiration, embolism, and pulmonary edema each need a different intervention, so the systematic search prevents fixation on the wrong cause.
Closed-loop communication with the surgeon (insufflation pressure, table angle, desufflation) is part of the treatment, not an afterthought.
questions: What were your first three actions when the SpO2 started to fall, and why in that order?
How did you exclude the anesthesia machine, circuit, and the SpO2 reading itself before treating the patient?
What was on your hypoxemia differential, and how did each finding move an item up or down the list?
What made this a right mainstem intubation, and why did insufflation and Trendelenburg cause it here?
Why withdraw rather than advance the tube, and how did you confirm you had it right?
After correcting the tube, why did oxygenation still need recruitment and PEEP, and what did you ask the surgeon to do?
How would you prevent this on the next steep-Trendelenburg laparoscopic case?
